title: Whether public squares should host commercial events or stay civic
summary: Argue whether the great public squares of a city should be rented out to markets, concerts, and brand activations, or kept as bare civic ground reserved for unprogrammed gathering.
topic: Whether public squares should host commercial events or remain unprogrammed civic ground
prompt: Write a 350–500-word argumentative essay about whether a city's central public square — somewhere like Plaza Mayor in Mexico City, Krakow's Rynek, Lisbon's Praça do Comércio, or any equivalent civic plaza you know — should be regularly rented out for Christmas markets, sponsored concerts, and brand activations, or whether it should be protected as unprogrammed civic ground. Use a 'what' cleft, a concession ('Granted,' or 'Admittedly,'), at least one passive construction, and one participle clause. Take a clear position. Anchor your argument in a specific square. Avoid both-sides-ism.
hintStarters: What a public square is for, beyond the obvious civic platitudes, is something city councils rarely have to defend until…
Granted, the revenue that a sponsored Christmas market generates for a cash-strapped municipality is not a trivial sum, and refusing it on principle can…
Standing in Krakow's Rynek the week after the summer concert stage has finally been dismantled, you notice how quickly the square remembers what it is for when…

hintVocab.meaningVi: sự kiện kích hoạt thương hiệu
lập trình / kín lịch một không gian
cho thuê không gian thuộc thành phố
chính quyền thành phố thiếu tiền
lời sáo rỗng về dân sự
đẩy đi những người thường lui tới
thương mại hóa không gian công cộng
cơ bắp dân sự / năng lực dân sự
sự bành trướng dần của lễ hội
quảng trường như phòng khách chung

sampleText:
Standing in Krakow's Rynek the week after the summer concert stage has finally been dismantled, you notice how quickly the square remembers what it is for when no one is selling anything in it. Pensioners sit on the church steps. Two boys kick a ball badly against a column. Someone is reading a newspaper out loud to a friend with weak eyes. None of this would be possible during the festival weeks, when ticketed enclosures and sponsor banners reduce the square to a corridor between fences.

What a public square is for, beyond the obvious civic platitudes, is something city councils rarely have to defend until the proposals to rent it out arrive. By then the language has already shifted. Officials speak of activation, footfall, and revenue per square metre. The square stops being described as ground and starts being described as inventory. Once that vocabulary settles in, the question of whether the square should be rented becomes a question of how often, and to whom.

Granted, the revenue that a sponsored Christmas market generates for a cash-strapped municipality is not a trivial sum, and refusing it on principle can come across as the luxury position of people who do not have to balance a city budget. Cities have to be paid for. Markets are old. Squares have hosted commerce for longer than most countries have existed, and the case for a totally event-free plaza ignores how mixed-use these spaces have always been.

Even so, the difference between a Tuesday vegetable market and a six-week pop-up sponsored by a tequila brand is not difficult to draw. The first is woven into the rhythm of the neighbourhood and disappears by mid-afternoon, leaving the square open. The second installs a barricaded perimeter, a stage, and a logo high enough to be seen from three streets away, and it returns the square to the public only after the contract ends. What gets rented out is not the floor — it is the civic muscle that the floor exists to support.

Admittedly, no square can be purely civic. People want to drink, dance, and buy small things outdoors, and a city that refuses every event in its central square will be accused, fairly, of preserving a museum. The argument is not against all programming. It is against the slow drift, encouraged by sponsorship decks and ambitious mayors, by which a square becomes booked more weeks than it is free, and in which spontaneous use — the protest, the chess game, the unexpected funeral procession — has nowhere to land.

A city that cannot point to one week in the year when its central square is doing nothing has, by that calendar alone, sold something it did not own.

sampleAnnotations.phrase: A city that cannot point to one week in the year when its central square is doing nothing has, by that calendar alone, sold something it did not own
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: Một thành phố không thể chỉ ra một tuần trong năm khi quảng trường trung tâm của nó không làm gì cả thì, chỉ qua lịch đó thôi, đã bán một thứ mà nó không sở hữu

sampleAnnotations.phrase: civic muscle
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: cơ bắp dân sự

sampleGrammarNotes.title: 'What' cleft to redefine what the square actually rents out
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What gets rented out is not the floor — it is the civic muscle that the floor exists to support' dùng cấu trúc 'What … is …' với cấu trúc 'not X — it is Y' để tái định nghĩa giao dịch. Bài luận đang lập luận rằng việc cho thuê quảng trường không phải là vật chất, mà là chức năng dân sự.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What gets rented out is not the floor — it is the civic muscle that the floor exists to support' uses a what-cleft with a 'not X — it is Y' structure to redefine the transaction. The essay is arguing that what is rented is not the physical surface but the civic function it performs.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Concession with 'Admittedly,' before an earned reversal
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'Admittedly, no square can be purely civic' mở một nhượng bộ thật trước khi quay lại lập luận chính. Bài luận không nói 'nhưng' ngay; nó dành cả một đoạn thừa nhận đối phương và sau đó định nghĩa lại lập luận một cách hẹp hơn ('The argument is not against all programming. It is against the slow drift…'). Cấu trúc 'not X. It is Y.' là một cách điển hình ở trình độ B2 để tinh chỉnh một luận điểm.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Admittedly, no square can be purely civic' opens a real concession before pivoting back to the main argument. The essay does not say 'but' immediately; it spends a whole paragraph granting the opponent's point and then redefines the argument more narrowly ('The argument is not against all programming. It is against the slow drift…'). The 'not X. It is Y.' structure is a B2-level tool for sharpening a thesis.

sampleTranslationVi:
Đứng ở quảng trường Rynek của Krakow trong tuần sau khi sân khấu hòa nhạc mùa hè cuối cùng đã được tháo dỡ, bạn nhận ra quảng trường nhớ ra mục đích của mình nhanh đến mức nào khi không có ai bán gì trong đó. Người về hưu ngồi trên bậc thềm nhà thờ. Hai cậu bé sút bóng vụng về vào một cây cột. Có ai đó đang đọc to một tờ báo cho một người bạn mắt yếu nghe. Không điều nào trong số này có thể xảy ra trong những tuần lễ hội, khi các khu vực có vé và biểu ngữ nhà tài trợ thu nhỏ quảng trường thành một hành lang giữa các hàng rào.

Mục đích của một quảng trường công cộng, vượt ra ngoài những lời sáo rỗng dân sự hiển nhiên, là điều mà hội đồng thành phố hiếm khi phải bảo vệ cho đến khi các đề xuất cho thuê nó được đưa ra. Khi ấy ngôn ngữ đã thay đổi. Các quan chức nói về kích hoạt, lượng người qua lại, và doanh thu trên mỗi mét vuông. Quảng trường ngừng được mô tả như mặt đất và bắt đầu được mô tả như hàng hóa tồn kho. Một khi từ vựng đó đã ổn định, câu hỏi liệu quảng trường có nên được cho thuê hay không trở thành câu hỏi cho thuê bao nhiêu lần và cho ai.

Công nhận, doanh thu mà một chợ Giáng sinh được tài trợ tạo ra cho một chính quyền thiếu tiền không phải là khoản tiền tầm thường, và từ chối nó trên nguyên tắc có thể bị xem như lập trường xa xỉ của những người không phải cân đối ngân sách thành phố. Các thành phố phải được chi trả. Chợ là cổ. Các quảng trường đã chứa thương mại lâu hơn hầu hết các quốc gia tồn tại, và lập luận cho một quảng trường hoàn toàn không có sự kiện bỏ qua việc những không gian này luôn luôn đa mục đích.

Dẫu vậy, sự khác biệt giữa một chợ rau ngày Thứ Ba và một sự kiện sáu tuần được tài trợ bởi một nhãn rượu tequila không khó để vạch ra. Cái đầu tiên được dệt vào nhịp điệu của khu phố và biến mất vào giữa chiều, để quảng trường mở rộng. Cái thứ hai lắp đặt một vành đai rào chắn, một sân khấu, và một logo đủ cao để được nhìn thấy từ ba con phố xa, và nó chỉ trả quảng trường về cho công chúng sau khi hợp đồng kết thúc. Cái được cho thuê không phải là mặt sàn — đó là cơ bắp dân sự mà mặt sàn tồn tại để hỗ trợ.

Thừa nhận rằng, không quảng trường nào có thể hoàn toàn mang tính dân sự. Người ta muốn uống, nhảy múa, và mua những thứ nhỏ ngoài trời, và một thành phố từ chối mọi sự kiện ở quảng trường trung tâm của mình sẽ bị buộc tội, một cách công bằng, là đang bảo tồn một bảo tàng. Lập luận không phải là chống lại mọi sự kiện được lập trình. Đó là chống lại sự trôi dạt chậm rãi, được khuyến khích bởi các hồ sơ tài trợ và các thị trưởng tham vọng, mà qua đó một quảng trường trở nên được đặt nhiều tuần hơn là nó tự do, và trong đó việc sử dụng tự phát — cuộc biểu tình, ván cờ, đám tang bất ngờ — không có nơi nào để hạ cánh.

Một thành phố không thể chỉ ra một tuần trong năm khi quảng trường trung tâm của nó không làm gì cả thì, chỉ qua lịch đó thôi, đã bán một thứ mà nó không sở hữu.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a 'what' cleft to redefine the transaction in renting a square?
options: What gets rented out is not the floor — it is the civic muscle that the floor exists to support.
The floor that the civic muscle exists to support is not rented out it is what.
Civic muscle the floor what gets rented out exists to support is not it.
It is what gets rented out the floor not — civic muscle is the support that exists.
mcQuestions.explanation: The cleft 'What … is …' delays the redefinition until after the dash, and the 'not X — it is Y' structure sharpens the moral contrast.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'What … is …' with a 'not X — it is Y' pivot.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to monetise public space' mean?
options: To clean up public space using money raised by fines
To turn public space into something that generates revenue, usually through rental, advertising, or ticketed events
To distribute money to people who use public space
To repaint coins in public squares
mcQuestions.explanation: Monetising public space means converting open civic ground into a source of income — through hire, sponsorship, or ticketed access.
mcQuestions.hint: What is the root verb in 'monetise'?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'Granted,' as a concession opener?
options: Granted, the revenue that a sponsored Christmas market generates for a cash-strapped municipality is not a trivial sum.
The revenue granted that a sponsored Christmas market for a cash-strapped municipality generates is not trivial sum a.
Sponsored Christmas market for a cash-strapped municipality the revenue is not a trivial sum granted that generates.
Is not a trivial sum the revenue granted that a sponsored Christmas market generates for a cash-strapped municipality.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Granted,' followed by a complete clause is the cleanest B2 concession opener.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option places 'Granted,' before a full, grammatical sentence?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which passive construction places the square in subject position to background the city's authorship?
options: The square stops being described as ground and starts being described as inventory.
Inventory and ground stops the square as being described.
As inventory the square ground starts being described as stops.
Being described as inventory the square starts as ground stops.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'is being described as ground / inventory' is a passive that puts 'the square' first and hides the official agents doing the describing.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option keeps 'The square' as subject and uses 'being described as'?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'festival creep' mean in the essay?
options: An unpleasant person who attends festivals
The slow drift by which more and more weeks of the year become booked for events, leaving less and less unprogrammed civic time
A small insect that infests festival tents
An advertising tactic at music festivals
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Creep' here is the gradual, unnoticed expansion of programming — what was once a weekend becomes weeks, what was once an exception becomes the rule.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of 'creep' as slow, almost invisible expansion.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence opens with a participle clause to set the analytical scene?
options: Standing in Krakow's Rynek the week after the summer concert stage has finally been dismantled, you notice how quickly the square remembers what it is for.
You notice standing in Krakow's Rynek the week after the summer concert stage has finally been dismantled how quickly the square remembers.
The square remembers standing in Krakow's Rynek how quickly what it is for the week after the summer concert stage has finally been dismantled.
The week after the summer concert stage standing in Krakow's Rynek finally been dismantled has you notice how quickly the square remembers what it is for.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Standing in Krakow's Rynek…' is a present-participle clause opener that sets the analytical scene before the main clause delivers the observation.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for an -ing phrase at the start of the sentence, separated by a comma from the main clause.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which transition best signals a return to the main argument after a concession?
options: For example, all Christmas markets are bad.
Even so, the difference between a Tuesday vegetable market and a six-week pop-up sponsored by a tequila brand is not difficult to draw.
On the contrary, no square has ever hosted commerce.
Furthermore, all sponsored events are heroic.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Even so' acknowledges the previous concession (squares have always hosted commerce) and pivots back to the writer's main argument by drawing a sharp distinction.
mcQuestions.hint: Which phrase both accepts the concession and reasserts the writer's position?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'spontaneous use' mean in the context of public squares?
options: Use that has been pre-approved by city council six months in advance
Use that arises without permits, without bookings, and without commercial sponsorship — the protest, the chess game, the funeral procession
Use that is led by professional event organisers
Use that requires a ticket to enter
mcQuestions.explanation: Spontaneous use is exactly what programmed and ticketed events crowd out: the unplanned, unbooked civic gatherings that need open ground to land.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the examples the essay gives — protest, chess game, funeral.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence maintains the most formal academic register?
options: Honestly, renting out squares is basically just selling your city's soul, no question.
It's kind of obvious that public squares shouldn't be full of sponsor banners all year, full stop.
The gradual conversion of central public squares into rentable event venues represents a structural rather than a cosmetic transformation of municipal space.
Squares are like, you know, supposed to be free and stuff for people to hang out.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option C uses formal vocabulary ('structural rather than cosmetic', 'municipal space') and avoids contractions, slang, and informal markers.
mcQuestions.hint: Avoid 'honestly', 'kind of', 'full stop', 'you know', and contractions.

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the essay's central position?
options: Public squares should be rented out year-round to maximise municipal revenue.
Public squares should host no events at all and remain entirely silent.
Public squares can host some commercial events but must retain enough unprogrammed time to support spontaneous civic use; a square that is never empty has been sold even when it is publicly owned.
Public squares should be replaced with private commercial plazas.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay defines the duty between two strawmen: not the museum-square that hosts nothing, and not the year-round venue, but the square that always retains real civic emptiness.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the final paragraph for the writer's specific verdict.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option best paraphrases 'reduce the square to a corridor between fences'?
options: Improve the square by adding shaded walkways
Make the square smaller and more usable for pedestrians
Strip the square of its open character and turn it into a narrow path bordered by event barriers
Build permanent fences across the entire square
mcQuestions.explanation: The phrase describes how event infrastructure shrinks the experience of the square from open civic ground to a narrow passage between commercial enclosures.
mcQuestions.hint: What does 'reduce X to Y' usually imply about X's previous fullness?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'civic muscle' mean as the essay uses it?
options: The strength of the construction crews who build city plazas
The capacity of a city's public life to gather, debate, mourn, and celebrate without commercial mediation
A literal muscle group used in civic exercise
The physical strength required to attend protest gatherings
mcQuestions.explanation: The 'muscle' is metaphorical — the square is what allows civic actions to happen at scale, and renting the square out weakens that capacity even when the floor is restored afterwards.
mcQuestions.hint: What does the square allow people to do collectively?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a defining relative clause correctly?
options: The revenue that a sponsored Christmas market generates for a cash-strapped municipality is not a trivial sum.
A sponsored Christmas market for a cash-strapped municipality the revenue is not generates a trivial sum that.
Generates a trivial sum not is the revenue that a sponsored Christmas market for a cash-strapped municipality.
Cash-strapped municipality the revenue generates that a sponsored Christmas market for a is not trivial sum.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'that a sponsored Christmas market generates for a cash-strapped municipality' is a defining relative clause specifying exactly which revenue the writer means.
mcQuestions.hint: Defining relative clauses follow the noun directly and start with 'who', 'that', or 'which'.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'enclose the commons' mean as a historical reference?
options: To build a roof over a public market
To take what was once shared, open public ground and turn it into private or commercial space that the public can no longer freely use
To put up Christmas lights around a town square
To organise a private dinner on public land
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Enclosure of the commons' is a historical term for the conversion of shared land into private property; the essay invokes it to describe what sponsored events do to civic squares.
mcQuestions.hint: What did the historical 'enclosure' do to shared land?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence closes the essay with a sharp, memorable verdict?
options: In conclusion, public squares are quite important to cities and we should think about them.
Therefore, all events in central squares must be banned by law immediately.
A city that cannot point to one week in the year when its central square is doing nothing has, by that calendar alone, sold something it did not own.
To sum up, both events and civic use are valuable in different ways.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option C closes with a sharp test (one empty week per year) that captures the essay's central moral claim about civic ownership.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option ends with a specific test the reader can apply to their own city?

mcQuestions.prompt: According to the essay, what is the strongest sign that a square has been over-programmed?
options: When pensioners refuse to attend the events held there
When the city's central square is booked more weeks of the year than it is free, leaving no time for unscheduled civic use
When the events held there do not generate enough revenue
When the events are organised by foreign brands rather than local ones
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay names the test directly: a square 'booked more weeks than it is free' has stopped functioning as civic ground.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at paragraph five for the writer's specific test of over-programming.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Think of a central public square in a city you know. How many weeks of the year is it doing nothing — no event, no market, no sponsorship — and just open for whoever turns up? What would you, as a council member, refuse to let the square be rented out for, and why?
